You have another stack of extra stuff starting on page 75 from an entirely different program. We're not going to talk about it, but I just want you to know you got it. We are beginning on page 84 with Copywriting Hijack number 13, which looks like this. And so when you think about what gets people's attention to Dan's point, what captivates their interest, when you think about something that has happened in media over time. So in the 1950s, all news content was completely separated with borders in between, meaning there was what is now called hard news, world affairs, national affairs at the local level, fires, robberies, killings, road closings, hard news. And a hard news cast dealt entirely with hard news. That's it. If you watched the Today Show in the David Garroway and Florence Henderson Black and White Days, the Today Show was a news show. It was not an entertainment show. Sports news was segmented. In the newspaper, of course, a sports section. And no one in sports was outside of that section. So if you get an Akron Bigger Journal and Akron Newspaper today, LeBron's on the front page. Never would have happened in the 1950s. I don't think there was going on. He'd be on the front page of the sports section. He wouldn't be on the front page of the main news section because he didn't belong there. Entertainment news was in the newspaper, the entertainment section. There were no entertainment-only TV shows like Entertainment Tonight, and that stuff all came along later. But there were gossip magazines. Right? So LA Confidential, you know, those kind of things. That's where that was. And it was entirely, everything was fenced off. And then gradually it began to kind of drift in what really is news. And all the way to where we are now, where not only is there complete overlap and no fences. Often, non-news is given priority over news news, even in what is purported to be a newscast. So the lead on the CNN Evening News might very well be Katy Perry's divorce or pregnancy or whatever it is that's going on with Katy Perry right now, before we even get to the fact that Russia invaded the Ukraine. And so like Muhammad Ali's death and funeral, right, which has dominated every news media there is. In 1950, that would have been sports news. All the way probably into 1970, it would have been profoundly segmented. It wouldn't have, for example, preempted the CBS Evening News to be live at Bill Clinton's speech at Muhammad Ali's funeral. It wouldn't have happened. So what it has done is it has turned all media into People Magazine. That's really what it's done. And people have now become ever more fascinated with people and less and less fascinated with everything else. So if you, so here's newsstand. So here's kind of what we would expect, but still the amount of it is amazing. So here's, this is just this week's stuff, last week's actually. So here's, I don't even know what the name of the damn magazine is, closer. And so the main deal is Robin Williams and what the world never knew about Robin Williams. And inside is Robin Williams, hidden wife, right? And also in that magazine under health update is what's her face from the Mary Tyler Moore show who has not yet died. And then, too soon, then we have In Touch and there we have Johnny and Amber's breakup and the $400 million divorce from hell and etc. And inside we have my friend Joan and how much she spent for stuff that is now being auctioned off at the SofaBees auction. And by the way, she could spend. Joan's joke about her place in New York, which is where all this stuff is from, is this is how Marie Antoinette would have lived if she had money. And so all this is real. I mean, $150,000 antique toilet and an $800 silver dog bowl and so on. And here's OK Magazine and we have the depth thing less important with them because they're pregnant. And then here we have Dep also inside their toxic marriage and here's why she's finally divorcing Lamar. All right, so it's all in those kind of magazines. The next time you're at a Barnes and Noble store, just check the whole newsstand. I chose not to buy one of everything, but I just bought one as an example. Here's Cigarro Fischiando magazine. Who's on the cover? Whatever name he's using this month. Yeah. But he could just as easily be over here and he is migrated to Cigarro Fischiando magazine. Forbes used to their number one issue every year was the 500 richest. No, the number one issue now is the hundred richest celebrities in the world. It outsells the other one by a three to one margin. If you go to Barnes and Noble newsstand and you work from left to right, my count roughly the other day was 30% of all magazines that have nothing whatsoever to do with people are all about people. They're not now the covers are not now about what they were about. It ain't about cigars. It's about him and it ain't about modern railroading. It's about what famous person has a Tory railroad in his basement. And so this is what people are into. Therefore, the foundation of persuasion is now that we are interested in people. So once we're interested in us and then what we're interested in most is somebody else, mostly somebody famous, but not necessarily. Therefore, if we are talking to them about anything else, we have something of a struggle on our hands. And that's why you see this change in these magazine covers is they gradually come to accept the fact that if they're showing anything else, they have an uphill struggle at selling a magazine. And why they've changed TV news and radio news because if we're not so look, it's why Trump's the God send of you know, to media because he's a damn celebrity. That's what's happened. They they're all getting a reality TV show for free. That's what they got. Right. And they use him and 12 million people tune in. They don't use him 800,000 tune in. When you think about so the debate, the worst of the debates pulled over 10 million viewers. The total viewership for primetime cable news is 2 million. That's all. More people watch entertainment tonight and TMZ added together than watch the news, by the way. So Trump is like, we want them all the time. The other day they were complaining. So there's a there's a Sunday morning news show. Fox has one and CNN was Fox. This is called media buzz. CNN's is reliable sources. Thank you very much. So these are show media shows about the media is what they are. So this is this is like having your own Facebook site and broadcast in the sandwich. So they now have an hour to talk about themselves and whether they're doing good media or bad media, you know, whatever. So they're peeing and moaning on reliable sources about all the media attention being given to Trump. Right. So half of the show is then complaining about Trump while they are talking about Trump and showing us pictures of Trump. Right. Well, why else? Right. And the big thing that they showed, which is funny. So Hillary was giving a speech. Why whatever night this was. And Bill was speaking somewhere and MSNBC, not just Fox MSNBC, Fox and CNN, all at the same time. They were showing none of those speeches. They were showing an empty stage with flags and a microphone waiting for Trump to arrive. This is what they were broadcasting is where he might get to sometime in the future. While these poor saps are actually speaking and nobody is covering the speeches. MSNBC was kind enough to put it in a little box down in the right-hand corner captioned Hillary speaking, but you couldn't hear her. You heard Brian Williams and Rachel Maddow talking about the empty stage with the microphone and the flags. And they were understandably unreliable sources aggravated about this. But it was their own network to do it because an empty stage where he might appear will hold the attention of people more than any of the other ones actually appearing. This is what has happened. Right. And so when you understand this is where we are at, whether you like it or not. So if you want to seize attention and hold attention, you have to think about people and then stories because this is where people are at. So here is a current control that is totally story driven. So here is a current control. I used to bike past this 200-year-old stone mansion as a kid and dream of living in a house like this. I never imagined that one day it would be mine. Let me share the story of how I built a seven-figure fortune. After I lost 62% of my savings and now live the life I always imagined. Now this is relatively daring in the sense that a lot of people are afraid to lead with a story. They are afraid that they will lose people before they get to make their pitch. So most people will lead with pitch elements and if they use a story they will get to it later. However, where we are now in many cases you are better off leading with the story because people are more interested in people than they are in anything else. The second thing that the people story does, I am on year 86, is it is one of those things that switches off somebody's critical thinking. This is important to understand. To Dan's point, so story triggers things in our brain that are different than what facts and figures and information and even ideas trigger. So it causes different emotional responses. But also it works differently than assertion. So when I assert X, everybody has a different reaction to that than when I present X in the context of a story about a person or about people. Outright assertion automatically causes a questioning response. We automatically push back against assertion. Well, who says so? Is that true? Do I agree with it? Is there a study? What are the facts? That doesn't sound right to me, etc., etc., etc. When it is put inside a story about a person, we don't do that. We get sucked into the story, if you will. And we are likely now to accept the premise when it is buried in a story. So you will see, they do it most of them clumsily. But you know, they've been doing it since Eisenhower, for God's sakes. And if you do that like a political event or the State of the Union speech, they will have the one-legged veteran or the headless college graduate or the single mom who has had to raise 12 children with no job. They'll have this person with them that they... So Hillary is going to be running around with Trump U victims, by the way. That's what she's going to be holding them up. And here I got Ivan here who mortgaged his grandma's wheelchair to take the coaching program and didn't get rich. And grandma now, they've had to bolt furniture casters on their feet and they push her around because she doesn't have a wheelchair anymore. And she's going to have the person right there. So when she tells you the story, she's going to be waving this pitiful person. So they all do it, right? All of them. On our side, their side, whatever side you're on, everybody does it. Every president at the State of the Union address sits two or three of these people next to the spouse up in the box. And at different times, has them stand up and tell the story about the one-legged veteran or the headless mom or whatever it is they've brought to the party. Why do they do that? Because that story works better than an outright assertion making the same point. Even people who were braced against their assertions get sucked into the story. And the story has a chance of changing their mind because it bypasses the mind and it triggers an emotional response. So that's the second reason to do them. So the term on page 87, there's two terms in this, it's from a book called Riveted. There's two terms that are real good to kind of lock on and think about and remember as you go ye forth to apply this stuff. One is social explanations. What a great term. So people now have a predilection. We know they want to know why. They want an explanation. But now what they like best is a social explanation. This is so because here's a story about a fireman that proves this point. Not an ex- here's a scientific explanation. Here's a factual explanation. Here's a mathematical explanation. Now social explanation now actually works better. Second great term. This is the popularity of horse groups as an example of our species centrism. What a great term. But he's right. So we are now totally centered on ourselves. And something Dan said is also true. We're training ourselves to be more and more centered on ourselves with that damn phone you have and all the social media. Everybody has decided they're interesting all of a sudden. And they're all broadcasting. Every moment. And so they are teaching people to do it in commercials. So the Arby's commercial has people taking pictures of the Arby's food they're eating and uploading it to their Facebook site. So it's going on in a commercial. Who gives a crap what you're eating. I'm like the most interesting guy in the room and people don't even care what I'm eating. What you're eating. But here we are. We're going to upload this taco. But what. But they're training people to do it. And so we are becoming more and more species centric. We are interested in us and them and nothing else. But it's horribly frustrating for example to the global warming advocates is nobody cares about the facts. Regardless of where you are on that thing. Nobody gives a rat's ass about the science. And they see they they should have kept on with the one picture they had of the polar bear stranded on the you know the one Gorg used the polar bear on the ice float out in the middle of nowhere who can't get back to shore like they swim. You know. Well but see people don't know. Say that thing played beautifully on people's ignorance. See they think it's the same as a brown bear. Right. They think those bears can only walk. Right. They swim like fish these things. Right. Plus they shot the picture like this as soon as you brought it out. You know there's a fishing boat over here and there's shore over there. But the picture of the stranded polar bear was much better than all the facts because we don't really care about facts. But we'll get sucked into a story. And people who are prone to reject assertions about global warming. Now you fo mystically called climate change because they realized it was hard to convince people of global warming when it was 22 degrees below and dilute. So we'll call it climate change. Right. So we no matter what the change is it's part of our crisis. But people who are prone to differ with the assertion. It's hard to resist the poor starving dying polar bear who is stuck on an ice flow out in the middle of nowhere because you use hairspray. That is a more compelling way to get there than to get there with assertion. Third thing this is now a more useful category of false proof because people are totally into it into social explanations. Social proof becomes ever more powerful. We can now prove cases purely with social proof. We don't need factual and scientific proof because people are centric to people. When you use this false proof there are four things to keep in mind. One is bulk. It loops to safety in numbers. The flood factor of here's a testimonial book with 5000 testimonials. Does anybody go read them. No. Doesn't matter. There's a flood factor to that. So quantity now quantity matters. Are they meaning do they have authority. Do they have credibility. Do they have believability. Are they are they relevant. So again now who they are. If it's a registered nurse how old is she how long has she been in our end. She specialized in anything. How do we give these people. So I did miracle there. I was looking for because we're some of the really old people. I'm looking for people that have testimonials and I'm throwing other ones out. I'm looking for ones that have high trust factor with old people. Pastors. School teachers firemen cops registered nurses. This football coach high school football coach. That's what I want. Because of this persuasiveness. Now what's the story. Do we have facts. Do we have details. Do we have specificity. Do we have photos. Do we have documents. Do we have. We're doing money. Do we're going to show tax returns. Are we going to show sworn statement by the accountant and then last affinity. Acceptance of people has proved goes up to the extent to which the people presented are just like me. This is again an argument for market segmentation. And showing different things to different people at different times. Because all the quantity in the world. And all the heft in the world. And what they say is undermined or helped by how close they are to me. Age family past occupation interests all of that people. So if you want to convince a fireman to do get rich in real estate show him fireman. You can show him cops. Not as good better than the general public. But fireman. Great. Perfect. Because he gets that. Just like me. His daily experience is just like my. So he's finding time to do this in between putting out fires and cooking chili and whatever the hell else they do.
